texto: It comes down to this. When we die, not only will our bodies be gone, but so will the people we remember. We live in the world, and we recall the world, and one day we won't do either anymore. The church bells will ring and the drunks will drink.
explicacao: Citação de Peter Orner sobre sentido da existência e experiência vivida. “It comes down to this. When we die, not only will our bodies be gone, but so will the people we r…” articula reflexao, death-and-dying, world em registro de reflexao, com relevância para contextos como luto e reflexao.

frase_original: It comes down to this. When we die, not only will our bodies be gone, but so will the people we remember. We live in the world, and we recall the world, and one day we won't do either anymore. The church bells will ring and the drunks will drink.

texto: Is this, Miriam wonders, what they call the march of history? And even if she doesn't fully understand, it doesn't mean she can't appreciate the need, the periodic need for some people to resort to gasoline, rags, and matches. Doesn't it always come to this? Isn't history as much about tearing things down as it is about building things up?
explicacao: Pergunta retórica de Peter Orner que provoca temas ligados a reflexao. Ao questionar “Is this, Miriam wonders, what they call the march of history? And even if she doesn't fully under…”, o texto convida o leitor a revisar crenças sobre reflexao, destruction, history, com foco em history e humanity.

Isn't history as much about tearing things down as it is about building things up?

texto: Chicago does not go to the world, the world comes to Chicago! Who needs New York? Who has taller buildings than our tall buildings? Who's got a busier airport than our airport? You want Picasso? We got Picasso, big Picasso. Nobody can make heads or tails of it. It's a lion? No, a seahorse. Looks to me like a radiator with wings. Who gives a damn, people, a Picasso's a Picasso.
explicacao: Metáfora atribuída a Peter Orner: compara elementos do cotidiano para falar de esforço, persistência e resultados. O trecho “Chicago does not go to the world, the world comes to Chicago! Who needs New York? Who has taller …” usa imagem concreta para lembrar que sucesso, chicago, city pedem movimento e interpretação pessoal, em tom de city e picasso.
